blurb: One of spring's quirkiest natives — nodding dusky-pink urn-shaped flowers give way to spectacular feathery, smoke-pink seedhead plumes over a low fern-leaved mat.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
seasons: Nodding pink flowers in spring, then smoky feathered seedheads; semi-evergreen ferny foliage

care.hardiness: Exceptionally cold-hardy, rated USDA zones 3–7, so it handles Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters easily; the limit here is summer heat and wet, not cold.[2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost, or early fall, in sharp-draining soil. In pots use a gritty alpine-style mix and keep it on the dry side, especially over winter — it is intolerant of wet winter conditions and rots in poorly drained ground.[2]
care.sun: Full sun in our climate (it accepts part shade in hotter regions), with good air circulation. The early spring bloom and feathery seed plumes color best in sun.[1][2]
care.soil: Lean, dry to medium, well-drained soil, near-neutral but adaptable; it grows on thin soil over limestone as well as clay and sand. On Boulder clay, plant high and add grit for drainage.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established but intolerant of wet, especially in winter. Avoid soggy sites where root rot sets in.[2]
care.spacing: Space about 9–12 inches apart; it slowly spreads by rhizomes into a low groundcover clump.[1][2]
care.propagation: Grown from seed or by division. Seed germinates without pretreatment, though cold stratification raises the germination rate. Because it spreads by rhizomes, mature clumps divide well — take rhizome cuttings or divisions in late summer or early spring.[1][2]
care.sow: Sow seed in late spring/early summer when ripe; no pretreatment is strictly necessary but stratification improves germination.[1]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratification is optional but increases the germination rate.[1]
care.bloom: Among the earliest perennials — nodding reddish-pink to purple bells in spring (roughly April–June), followed by the smoky, feathery seed plumes that give it its name.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Low; let the ornamental seed plumes stand, and divide every few years to renew. Mainly needs sharp drainage to thrive.[2]
care.troubles: Root rot in poorly drained or wet-winter soils is the chief problem; otherwise trouble-free.[2]

references.name: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Geum triflorum
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Geum triflorum

edible.summary: No culinary use; only a traditional medicinal root tea is documented, so it is treated as inedible.[2]
edible.caution: Native peoples once boiled the roots for a medicinal tea, but no part has documented food use — this is a medicinal/ethnobotanical use, not a food, so don't eat it. Not notably poisonous, but not a forage plant.[2]
